blocks.text: Onboarding Record: Jason Jordan
Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Jason Jordan joins Brackenridge Civil Group Inc as Design Technician in the Engineering group. It is set down here so that there is no disagreement later about what was said at the beginning. I have written it myself rather than have it typed from notes, because the expectations set out in it are mine and he is entitled to have them in the words I would use.

The work is plan production. Site grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility drawings leave this office to a single standard, and the technician's seat is where that standard is kept: layering, line weights, the title block, the sheet index, and the plotting setup that turns a drawing file into a check print an engineer can mark in red. Jason Jordan comes to us already knowing that work, and knowing it is the reason he was hired rather than someone we would have had to teach it to. On any given job he takes direction on the sheets from the engineer running it. He reports to me.

He will not be handed a sheet of his own for the first several weeks. He will check prints against markups, sit with the engineers long enough to hear what a sheet is meant to say before he is asked to make it say it, and read the standards folder end to end. The office opens at seven-thirty and keeps regular hours. Time is recorded against a job number the day it is worked, not reconstructed on a Friday afternoon. Drawing files belong in the lettered project folder on the share and nowhere else: not on the local machine, not on a diskette in a desk drawer. Where the standards do not answer a question, he is to bring it to me. I wrote most of them, and I will defend them or change them on the spot.

Two matters I will state plainly, because neither is a formality. The first is that a plan set going out under a sealed cover sheet carries this firm's name and my seal, and it is checked before it goes, whatever the schedule is said to require. The second is that if Jason Jordan does not know something, he is to ask before he draws it rather than after. This office is small enough that asking costs nothing. Guessing has a way of costing a great deal, and it never costs it quietly. I am glad to have him here.
Firm Overview

Brackenridge Civil Group Inc is a civil engineering practice serving municipal and county public works departments, public water and sewer authorities, and private development clients. The firm's product is a sealed set of construction drawings and the specifications that accompany them, prepared by the people whose names are on them and read by the Principal Engineer before it leaves the office. We are a small practice by intention. The client who retains this firm deals with the engineer who does the work, and with no one else.

Our practice is site civil design in the ordinary sense of the term: what happens to the ground, to the water that lands on it, and to the pipe and pavement that have to coexist above and beneath it. We do not hold ourselves out as structural engineers, as environmental consultants, or as planners. Where a project requires those disciplines we say so early and in writing, and we coordinate with the specialist the client selects.
Services

items: Site grading and earthwork design, including cut and fill balance, quantity takeoffs, and erosion and sediment control plans and narrative.

Stormwater management: drainage area analysis, hydrologic and hydraulic calculations, conveyance and inlet design, detention and water quality facilities, and the supporting narrative required for permit review.
Roadway design: horizontal and vertical geometry, typical sections, intersection detail, signing and striping plans, and maintenance of traffic.
Utility design: water distribution and sanitary sewer collection, gravity and pressure profiles, and the resolution of utility conflicts on paper rather than in the field.
Bid phase services: preparation of bid documents, addenda, tabulation of bids, and recommendation of award.
Construction phase services: shop drawing review, responses to contractor requests for information, periodic site observation, and record drawings.

blocks.text: Drawings are produced in this office on our own CAD standard, checked against a print, and issued under the seal of the Principal Engineer. Specifications are prepared to the owner's front end where the owner has one, and to the applicable state and county standard construction details where the owner does not.
What a Client Can Expect

A public body that retains this firm is buying a set of drawings that will survive review, a set of quantities the low bidder will not dispute, and a telephone call returned the same day. That is the whole of our claim. We do not promise a design that costs less than the ground will allow. We do not agree to a review schedule we already know the reviewing agency will not meet. Where we believe a scope is wrong, we say so during the proposal rather than during construction, when saying so is a change order and no one is pleased to hear it.
Representative Work

The firm is presently engaged by {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}. That work is of the kind described above, and it is being performed in this office rather than brokered to a subconsultant and administered from a distance. A prospective client is welcome to speak with {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}} about our performance rather than accept our characterization of it, and we will make that introduction on request. We would sooner be judged on an engagement we can point to than on a list.
How the Work Is Run

Mark Todd, Principal Engineer, is responsible for the technical content of everything this firm issues. He reads every agreement and every letter to a public body line by line before it is signed, and he signs them. Design and plan production are carried out by the firm's engineering staff to the standards described above, all of it in-house. Mark Barrett, Office Administrator, maintains the firm's project files, correspondence, and contract administration. Executed agreements are scanned and filed the day they come back countersigned, and a client who telephones for a copy of a document from one of our jobs will have it that day rather than after a search.
Inquiries

Inquiries regarding the firm's availability, its rates, or a specific project should be directed to Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. We will contact you promptly. Proposals are prepared at no cost to the prospective client and are written to the scope that was described to us; we do not publish a standard fee schedule and then negotiate away from it.
Onboarding Record: Jeffrey Perez
Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Jeffrey Perez has accepted the position of Design Technician and joins the Engineering group. He comes in at the quiet end of the year, which suits me. There is time to put him through the standards and the detail library before the spring work arrives, and I intend to use all of it.

His work will be details and quantity takeoffs, with plan production as the balance of it. I want to be direct with him about why that assignment matters more than the title suggests. A takeoff is the number a contractor prices. A quantity that is wrong is not a drafting error somebody catches on the check print; it is a bid error, and it comes back to this office months later wearing a different name and asking for money. Jeffrey Perez has no wish to be the reason that happens. That disposition is most of the reason he is sitting here, and I would rather have it than speed.

For his first weeks he will work against sets that have already been issued rather than live ones. He is to take off quantities from those sheets, compare his numbers against what was tabulated at the time, and bring me every difference, including the ones he believes are his own error. Especially those. Where a detail does not make sense to him he is to ask what it is for, not merely what it ought to look like. I am told he asks a great many questions. He will find that welcome here and he should not moderate it on my account, though I would ask that he pick his moment when an engineer is on the telephone with a reviewer.

The ordinary matters, briefly. Time is booked to a job number the day it is worked. Drawing files live in the lettered project folder on the share and are not kept on a local drive. Anything that leaves this office does so over my signature and after my review, which is not a remark on Jeffrey Perez but the standing rule and applies to work of mine as readily as to his. We are glad to have him.
Onboarding Record: Charles Olson
Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Charles Olson joins Brackenridge Civil Group Inc as Design Technician in the Engineering group. He came up on the board. That is worth setting down in the record because it is becoming uncommon and because it shows in the work. A man who has drawn a profile by hand understands what the profile is for. The software has not yet found a way to teach that, and I am not persuaded it is trying.

His assignment is plan production across our grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility work, to the office CAD standard. I expect he will find the standards agreeable; they were written by people who also came up on the board, and they are stricter than they need to be for that reason. Where he finds one of them wrong, he is to tell me so directly. I would far rather hear it from the man who has drawn the sheet than from a reviewing agency in a comment letter.

One expectation I am putting in writing, because it is the reason I hired him. Charles Olson would rather walk the site than take the survey file's word for what is out there. That instinct is correct, and I do not want it trained out of him by schedule pressure. When a plan looks too tidy to be the ground, it is generally the plan that is wrong and not the ground. He is authorized to go and look, on his own judgment, without clearing it with me first. Site time is booked to the job like any other hour. What I ask in return is this: when he comes back with something the survey did not show, it is written down the same day and it goes in the project folder. Short and concrete, in whatever words he would use to say it out loud, is exactly the right length. A note nobody wrote is a note this firm does not have, and we find that out at the worst possible moment or not at all.

The rest is ordinary. Files stay in the lettered project folder on the share. Time is recorded against a job number daily. Nothing goes out under seal that I have not read. I have said all of this to Charles Olson in person; it is set down here so that it is on the record, and so that he can hold me to the part about walking the site.
Firm Overview

Brackenridge Civil Group Inc is a civil engineering firm practicing site grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility design. Our clients are public works departments, water and sewer authorities, and private developers building in this county and the ones adjoining it. The firm is small by intention. There is no business development staff here. The engineer who signs the proposal is the engineer who does the work, and the engineer who takes the telephone call two years afterward when the contractor hits something nobody drew.

The scope of our practice has not changed since the firm opened and there is no plan to change it. We design what happens at grade: the movement of earth, the collection and conveyance of stormwater, the geometry of pavement, and the placement of pipe that has to fit beneath both. Work outside that scope is referred rather than attempted. Clients occasionally find that frustrating. They find it less frustrating than the alternative.
Services and Deliverables

rows: Grading plans, cut and fill quantities, erosion and sediment control plans and narrative
Drainage area mapping, hydrologic and hydraulic calculations, conveyance and inlet design, detention and water quality facilities, permit narrative
Horizontal and vertical geometry, typical sections, intersection details, signing and striping, maintenance of traffic
Water distribution and sanitary collection plans, gravity and pressure profiles, utility conflict resolution
Bid documents, addenda, tabulation of bids, recommendation of award
Shop drawing review, responses to requests for information, periodic site observation, record drawings

blocks.text: Every sheet is produced in this office on our own CAD standard, checked against a print, and issued under the seal of the Principal Engineer. Nothing leaves the office that he has not read, and the schedule is not accepted as a reason to make an exception, because the schedule is always the reason offered.
Clients

The firm's continuing engagement is with {{fact:f:E-2001-001.client}}, for whom this office has served as civil engineer of record on work of the kind tabulated above. That is the relationship we would ask a prospective client to check, and we will put you in touch on request rather than offer our own account of it.

The firm is also engaged by {{fact:f:E-2004-001.client}}. Both engagements are staffed out of this office. We do not broker work to a subconsultant and then administer it from here; if the drawing carries our seal, it was drawn in this room by someone whose name is on it.

We are aware that two clients is a short list. It is short because we have not chased volume, because we do not take work outside the scope described above, and because we do not count a proposal as a client. What we can tell a prospective client is that neither of the two has had occasion to go looking for another civil engineer.
Staffing and Responsibility

Mark Todd, Principal Engineer, is responsible for the technical content of everything this firm issues and is the signatory on every agreement and every letter to a public body. Design and plan production are carried out by the firm's engineering staff, all of it in-house. The firm's project files are maintained by our administration in lettered project folders, and a client who telephones for a copy of an executed agreement or a transmittal from a closed job will have it the same day.
Terms of Engagement

We propose against a defined scope, at a lump sum where the scope will support one and on an hourly basis where it will not, and we say which in the proposal rather than leave the client to discover it in the first invoice. Where we believe a scope is wrong, we say so before the agreement is executed. Additional services are proposed in writing and performed after authorization, never before it. We have not yet sent a client an invoice they had not already agreed to receive, and we intend to keep that record intact.
Inquiries

Correspondence and inquiries concerning the firm's availability or a specific project should be addressed to Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. We will contact you within the week. Proposals are prepared at no cost and are written to the scope described to us.
Onboarding Record: Kimberly Branch
Prepared by Mark Todd, Principal Engineer. Original to the personnel file, copy to the employee.

Kimberly Branch joins the firm as Design Technician in the Engineering group. It is written in the same form as the ones before it, which is to say plainly and without a great deal of encouragement in it. She did not ask for any in the interview, and I do not believe she requires it.

The assignment is plan production across the firm's grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility work, drawn to the office CAD standard. She said in the interview that she prefers to understand what a sheet is meant to accomplish before she draws it, and to ask the engineer directly rather than infer it from the markup. That is how it is done here, and it is how it has always been done here. No engineer in this office is entitled to be put out by that question. If one of them is, she should tell me, and I will settle it.

She should be warned about something the standards folder will not tell her. This office has conventions that are written down nowhere: how a particular agency wants its sheets numbered, which detail we substitute for the state standard and the reason we do, what the plotter does on the third sheet if nobody is watching it. Those conventions live in the heads of the people who have been here a while, and they will be handed to her one at a time and out of order, usually at the moment they are needed and not before. My advice, and it is advice rather than instruction, is that she write each one down as she collects it. Whatever she builds that way will belong to her, and I expect she will find it worth more than the standards folder is.

Two rules to close on. Time is booked to a job number the day it is worked, and drawing files stay in the lettered project folder on the share. And the name of a public agency is spelled correctly in the title block, every time, checked against the executed agreement and not against memory. This firm has never sent a set out the door with a client's name wrong on the cover. I would like that record to survive the arrival of Kimberly Branch. I say the same thing to everyone, so she should read nothing into it.
